extr 0.9.0

Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model

extinction_probability_wz_di.Rd

Computes the extinction probability of a density-independent stochastic population model based on the given w and z statistics.

Usage

extinction_probability_wz_di(w, z)

Arguments

w

numeric: w statistic for extinction probability calculation.

z

numeric: z statistic for extinction probability calculation.

Value

numeric: Estimated extinction probability.

Details

Let \(T\) be a random variable representing the time at which the population becomes extinct. The extinction probability within a given time horizon \(t_h\) is defined as \(\Pr[T \leq t_h]\).

Hakoyama (2025) expressed this extinction probability as a function of two transformed statistics, \(w\) and \(z\), yielding the following formula: $$ \Pr[T \leq t_h] = G(w, z) = \Phi(-w) + \exp{\left(\frac{z^2 - w^2}{2}\right)} \Phi(-z), $$ where \(\Phi(\cdot)\) is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution.

For large values of \(z\) (typically \(z \geq 35\)), this exact formula may suffer from numerical inaccuracies. Dennis et al. (1991) derived an asymptotic approximation for extinction probabilities expressed directly in terms of \(\mu\), \(\sigma^2\), \(n_q\), \(n_e\), and \(t_h\).

This package reformulates their approximation in terms of \(w\) and \(z\), resulting in the following asymptotic expansion: $$ G(w, z) \approx \Phi(-w) + \exp{\left(-\frac{w^2}{2}\right)} \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \left(\frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{z^3} + \frac{3}{z^5} - \frac{15}{z^7} + \frac{105}{z^9} - \frac{945}{z^{11}} + \frac{10395}{z^{13}}\right). $$

This approximation improves numerical stability while maintaining high accuracy for large \(z\).

See also

w_statistic, z_statistic

Author

Hiroshi Hakoyama, hiroshi.hakoyama@gmail.com
